1: τήν κλεῖν τῆς ἀβύσσου — the key of the Abyss: the Abyss (abyssos) is the demonic underworld-prison (Revelation 9:1–2, 11; Revelation 11:7; Revelation 17:8); the key signals total authority over access. Christ holds "the keys of Death and Hades" (Revelation 1:18); this angel acts with delegated authority from the one who holds all keys. ἅλυσιν μεγάλην — a great chain: the imagery of supernatural binding echoes 1 Enoch Revelation 18:16; Revelation 21:3–6 (fallen angels bound in chains of darkness in a place of desolation); Jewish apocalyptic angelology provided the conceptual framework for constraining demonic powers.
2: ἐκράτησεν τόν δράκοντα — seized the dragon (aorist: a single decisive act); the fourfold identification follows: ho ophis ho archaios (the ancient serpent = Gen 3), ho diabolos (the devil = slanderer/accuser, the Greek translation of Hebrew satan), ho Satanas (Satan = the adversary). All four titles converge on one entity, exposing the continuity of the enemy across the entire biblical narrative. χίλια ἔτη — a thousand years: the term "millennium" derives from Latin mille (thousand) + annum (year); whether the period is literal or symbolic is the central dispute between premillennialism, amillennialism, and postmillennialism; Revelation's Greek does not adjudicate this debate but frames the binding as purposeful (Revelation 20:3: so that he could no longer deceive the nations) and temporary.
3: ἔβαλεν ... ἔκλεισεν ... ἐσφράγισεν — threw, shut, sealed (three aorists in sequence): the three verbs indicate progressive intensity of confinement; the sealing (sphragizō) echoes Matthew 27:66 (the sealing of Jesus's tomb by the authorities) — ironic reversal: the one whose tomb was sealed now seals the Abyss. ἵνα μή πλανήσῃ τά ἔθνη ἔτι — so that he might no longer deceive the nations: planaō (to lead astray, deceive) is the dragon's defining activity throughout Revelation (Revelation 12:9; Revelation 20:8, 10); the binding is purposeful — neutralizing the deceiver's capacity to mislead. δεῖ αὐτόν λυθῆναι μικρόν χρόνον — it is necessary for him to be released for a little while: the divine dei (must/it is necessary) frames even the devil's release as serving the divine purpose.
4: κρίμα ἐδόθη αὐτοῖς — judgment was given to them (divine passive: God granted judicial authority): the saints who were judged by earthly tribunals now hold judicial authority themselves; the reversal of Daniel 7:22 (judgment was given for the holy ones of the Most High). τάς ψυχάς τῶν πεπελεκισμένων — the souls of those beheaded (pelekizō = to behead with an axe; only here in the NT): the axe-beheading was the Roman method of capital punishment for condemned enemies of the state; the specific instrument of the martyrs' deaths becomes the credential for their co-reign. ἔζησαν καί ἐβασίλευσαν μετά τοῦ Χριστοῦ χίλια ἔτη — they lived and reigned with Christ for a thousand years: ezēsan (they lived = resurrection verb); the first resurrection is participation in Christ's reign.
5: οἱ λοιποί τῶν νεκρῶν οὐκ ἔζησαν ἄχρι τελεσθῇ τά χίλια ἔτη — the rest of the dead did not live until the thousand years were completed: the two-stage resurrection implied here — the martyred saints reign first (Revelation 20:4, 6 = the "first resurrection"), then the general resurrection at the end (Revelation 20:12–13) before the great white throne. αὕτη ἡ ἀνάστασις ἡ πρώτη — this is the first resurrection: the definite article and demonstrative (hautē = this one specifically) identify what was just described (the living-and-reigning of the martyrs with Christ) as "the first resurrection"; the term is unique to this passage in the NT.
6: μακάριος καί ἅγιος ὁ ἔχων μέρος ἐν τῇ ἀναστάσει τῇ πρώτῃ — blessed and holy is the one having a share in the first resurrection: the fifth beatitude of Revelation; makarios (blessed, in the condition of divine favor) + hagios (holy, set apart for God) together denote the eschatological status of those in the first resurrection. ὁ δεύτερος θάνατος οὐκ ἔχει ἐξουσίαν ἐπ' αὐτούς — the second death has no authority over them: exousia (authority, delegated power); the second death (defined at 20:14 as the lake of fire) is itself personified as having authority that it exercises over the condemned — but not over those who share the first resurrection. ἱερεῖς τοῦ θεοῦ καί τοῦ Χριστοῦ — priests of God and of Christ: the priestly identity of the redeemed (Exodus 19:6; Revelation 1:6; Revelation 5:10) extends through the millennium — direct, unmediated access to the divine presence.
7: ὅταν τελεσθῇ τά χίλια ἔτη — when the thousand years are completed (aorist subjunctive: at the divinely fixed point of completion; teleō = to bring to its designed end): the duration is not open-ended but has a purpose-defined terminus. λυθήσεται ὁ Σατανᾶς ἐκ τῆς φυλακῆς αὐτοῦ — Satan will be released from his prison (future passive: the same divine agency that imprisoned also releases, for purposes that serve the plan — cf. Revelation 20:3's dei [it is necessary]). The prison language (phylakē) frames the Abyss as a detention, not a permanent sentence; the final judgment of 20:10 is the permanent sentence.
8: πλανῆσαι τά ἔθνη — to deceive the nations (aorist infinitive of purpose): Satan's resumed activity after release is identical to his pre-binding activity (planaō = to lead astray; cf. Revelation 12:9; Revelation 20:3, 10); the millennium's end does not reform Satan. τόν Γώγ καί Μαγώγ — Gog and Magog: from Ezek 38–39 where Gog of Magog leads a great coalition against the restored Israel; in Revelation these names function symbolically for all anti-God forces gathered for the final assault (cf. Sib. Or. 3.319, 512). ὁ ἀριθμός αὐτῶν ὡς ἡ ἄμμος τῆς θαλάσσης — their number like the sand of the sea: the Abrahamic covenant promise (Genesis 22:17: descendants like the sand of the sea) is grotesquely parodied; the final anti-God army matches the promised people in number.
9: ἀνέβησαν ἐπί τό πλάτος τῆς γῆς — they went up over the breadth/width of the earth: platos (breadth) implies the army spreads comprehensively across all the earth. τήν παρεμβολήν τῶν ἁγίων καί τήν πόλιν τήν ἠγαπημένην — the camp of the holy ones and the beloved city: parembolē (military encampment; the people of God figured as the wilderness camp of Exodus); hē ēgapēmenē polis (the beloved city) echoes Psalms 78:68; 87:2 for Jerusalem — the eschatological people of God figured both as a mobile camp and a beloved city. κατέβη πῦρ ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καί κατέφαγεν αὐτούς — fire came down from God out of heaven and consumed them: no battle, no struggle; the divine fire alone destroys the vast army in a moment (Elijah's pattern, 2 Kings 1:10–12, at cosmic scale).
10: ὁ διάβολος ὁ πλανῶν αὐτούς ἐβλήθη εἰς τήν λίμνην τοῦ πυρός καί θείου — the devil who had been deceiving them was thrown into the lake of fire and sulfur (theion = sulfur/brimstone; the sulfurous fire recalls the destruction of Sodom, Genesis 19:24, and the judgment oracles of Isaiah 30:33; Revelation 34:9). ὅπου καί τό θηρίον καί ὁ ψευδοπροφήτης — where also the beast and the false prophet: already there since Revelation 19:20; the unholy trinity (dragon + beast + false prophet = Satan + political power + religious propaganda) converge in the same final state. βασανισθήσονται ἡμέρας καί νυκτός εἰς τοὺς αἰῶνας τῶν αἰώνων — will be tormented day and night forever and ever: the strongest formulation of duration available in Greek, matching the eternal smoke of Revelation 14:11.
11: θρόνον λευκόν μέγαν — a great white throne: white (leukos) throughout Revelation signals holiness, victory, and divine purity; this throne is neither the twenty-four elders' thrones (Revelation 4:4) nor the beast's throne (Revelation 16:10) but the supreme judicial tribunal. ἔφυγεν ἡ γῆ καί ὁ οὐρανός ἀπό τοῦ προσώπου αὐτοῦ — earth and heaven fled from his face: the dissolution of the present created order before the Judge; the flight of heaven and earth anticipates the new heaven and new earth (Revelation 21:1) and signals the inadequacy of the present order to sustain direct divine presence. τόπος οὐχ εὑρέθη αὐτοῖς — no place was found for them (heurethē = divine passive: was found); the old creation has nowhere to exist in the presence of the holy Judge.
12: νεκρούς τούς μεγάλους καί τούς μικρούς — the dead the great and the small: universal scope; no social hierarchy, no temporal power, no religious status alters standing before this throne. βιβλία ἠνοίχθησαν — books were opened (divine passive): the heavenly records of human deeds; cf. Daniel 7:10 ("the books were opened") in the Son-of-Man vision. βίβλος τῆς ζωῆς — the book of life: the single book of life stands alongside the many books of works; throughout Revelation the book of life is the register of those who belong to the Lamb (Revelation 3:5; Revelation 13:8; Revelation 17:8; Revelation 20:12, 15; Revelation 21:27). ἐκρίθησαν κατά τά ἔργα αὐτῶν — they were judged according to their works: the works are the evidence of character and allegiance, not the meritorious basis of salvation; the book of life (grace) and the books of works (accountability) together constitute the judgment.
13: ἔδωκεν ἡ θάλασσα τούς νεκρούς τούς ἐν αὐτῇ — the sea gave up the dead in it: the sea was a place of the dead in ancient cosmology (Psalms 88:5–6; Job 26:5); death at sea — with no recoverable body — does not exempt from resurrection. ὁ θάνατος καί ὁ ᾅδης ἔδωκαν — Death and Hades gave up: Death and Hades are personified powers (Revelation 6:8; Revelation 20:14) that temporarily hold the dead; at the general resurrection, they must surrender every person they hold. The threefold source of the dead (sea, Death, Hades) is comprehensive — every category of the dead is raised for judgment. κατά τά ἔργα αὐτῶν — according to their works: the refrain of universal accountability.
14: ὁ θάνατος καί ὁ ᾅδης ἐβλήθησαν εἰς τήν λίμνην τοῦ πυρός — Death and Hades were thrown into the lake of fire (aorist passive: divine act): the personified powers of death are themselves subjected to final judgment; after the general resurrection, they have no more function and are permanently destroyed. οὗτος ὁ θάνατος ὁ δεύτερός ἐστιν ἡ λίμνη τοῦ πυρός — this is the second death, the lake of fire: the definitional statement; ho thanatos ho deuteros (the second death) has precedent in Aramaic/rabbinic tradition (Targum on Deuteronomy 33:6; y. Ber. Revelation 9:3; the concept of a "second death" for the wicked appears in pre-Christian Aramaic texts), making the term likely familiar to John's Jewish-background readers.
15: εἴ τις οὐχ εὑρέθη ἐν τῇ βίβλῳ τῆς ζωῆς γεγραμμένος — if anyone was not found written in the book of life: heurethē (divine passive: was found by the divine searcher); gegramenos (perfect passive participle: having been written, enrolled in the permanent record — the perfect tense emphasizing the enduring state of enrollment). The conditional form (ei tis = if anyone) applies the criterion universally without naming individuals. ἐβλήθη εἰς τήν λίμνην τοῦ πυρός — was thrown into the lake of fire (aorist passive: the final judicial sentence): the passive voices throughout the judgment sequence (was found, was judged, was thrown) keep divine agency central while the human record (the books of works) provides the evidentiary basis.
